title: Semi-Supervised Learning
description: Learning from labeled + unlabeled data: self-training, co-training, pseudo-labeling, consistency regularization, and graph-based methods.
details: Semi-supervised learning (SSL) uses unlabeled data alongside limited labeled data to build better models. This is the most common real-world scenario — labels are expensive (expert radiologists, manual annotation), but unlabeled data is abundant. This file covers self-training (pseudo-labeling with confidence thresholds), co-training (multiple views of the same data), consistency regularization (FixMatch, MixMatch, UDA), graph-based methods (label propagation, graph neural networks), and entropy minimization. SSL is a core topic at FAANG ML interviews, especially for roles involving data-limited domains like healthcare, autonomous driving, and NLP.

sections.title: Self-Training & Pseudo-Labeling
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Train a model on labeled data. Use it to predict labels for unlabeled data. Add the “pseudo-labeled” samples with high confidence back into the training set. Repeat. It's like a student who studies a few solved problems, then tries to solve unsolved ones, then studies the solutions they're confident about.

Self-training algorithm:

  • Step 1: Train classifier on labeled data L.
  • Step 2: Predict pseudo-labels for unlabeled data U: ŷ = fθ(x).
  • Step 3: Select samples with max confidence P(ŷ | x) > T (threshold, e.g., 0.95). Add (x, ŷ) to labeled set L.
  • Step 4: Retrain model on augmented labeled set.
  • Step 5: Repeat until no samples exceed threshold or max iterations reached.

Key considerations:

  • Confirmation bias: The model reinforces its own mistakes — wrong pseudo-labels get amplified. Mitigation: use a high confidence threshold, curriculum learning (increase threshold over time), or multiple models.
  • Threshold scheduling: Start with a low threshold (include easy samples), gradually increase. This lets the model learn from easy cases before tackling hard ones.
  • Hard vs soft labels: Hard labels (one-hot) push the model to be more confident. Soft labels (predicted probabilities) preserve uncertainty. Soft labels often work better.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# === Self-Training ===
def self_training(X_labeled, y_labeled, X_unlabeled, model,
                  threshold=0.95, max_iter=100):
    """
    Simple self-training with pseudo-labeling.
    """
    X_l, y_l = X_labeled.copy(), y_labeled.copy()
    X_u = X_unlabeled.copy()
    
    for iteration in range(max_iter):
        model.fit(X_l, y_l)
        
        if len(X_u) == 0:
            break
        
        # Predict probabilities on unlabeled
        proba = model.predict_proba(X_u)
        confidence = proba.max(axis=1)
        pseudo_labels = proba.argmax(axis=1)
        
        # Select high-confidence samples
        confident = confidence >= threshold
        
        if not confident.any():
            break  # nothing new to add
        
        # Add to labeled set
        X_l = np.vstack([X_l, X_u[confident]])
        y_l = np.concatenate([y_l, pseudo_labels[confident]])
        X_u = X_u[~confident]
        
        print(f'Iteration {iteration}: added {confident.sum()} samples')
    
    return model

# === Usage ===
# Split data into labeled (10%) and unlabeled (90%)
X_labeled, X_unlabeled, y_labeled, y_unlabeled = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.9, stratify=y, random_state=42
)

model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200, random_state=42)
final_model = self_training(X_labeled, y_labeled, X_unlabeled, model)

# === Soft pseudo-labels ===
# Instead of hard labels, use predicted probabilities
# The loss uses soft labels: L = -sum(proba_pred * log(proba_true))
# This preserves uncertainty and reduces confirmation bias

# === Scikit-learn has built-in SelfTrainingClassifier ===
# from sklearn.semi_supervised import SelfTrainingClassifier
# base_model = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=200)
# self_train = SelfTrainingClassifier(base_model, threshold=0.95, max_iter=10)
# self_train.fit(X_combined, y_combined)  # y_combined has -1 for unlabeled

sections.title: Co-Training
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Split features into two “views” (e.g., text content vs metadata). Train a separate model on each view. Each model's confident predictions become labeled data for the other model. The two models bootstrap each other — like two students learning from different textbooks who teach each other what they're confident about.

Co-training assumptions:

  • Sufficient and redundant views: Each view alone is sufficient for classification, and the views are conditionally independent given the class label. Example: web page classification — features from the page content and features from links pointing to it. Both views predict the topic independently.
  • When views exist naturally: Audio + video (speaker recognition), protein sequence + structure, product image + description.
  • When views don't exist: Create them artificially — randomly split features, use different model architectures, or different preprocessing.

Co-training algorithm:

  • Step 1: Train f₁ on View 1 labeled data, f₂ on View 2 labeled data.
  • Step 2: Each model predicts pseudo-labels on unlabeled data (its own view).
  • Step 3: Each model selects its most confident predictions.
  • Step 4: For each selected sample, view 1 provides its label prediction, and the sample (with both views) is added to the other model's training set.
  • Step 5: Retrain both models and repeat.
sections.codeBlock:
import numpy as np
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

# === Co-Training ===
def cotraining(X1_labeled, X2_labeled, y_labeled,
               X1_unlabeled, X2_unlabeled,
               base_model=SVC(kernel='linear', probability=True),
               threshold=0.9, n_samples_per_iter=10, max_iter=50):
    """
    Co-training with two views of the data.
    X1: features for view 1
    X2: features for view 2
    """
    X1_l, X2_l = X1_labeled.copy(), X2_labeled.copy()
    y_l = y_labeled.copy()
    X1_u, X2_u = X1_unlabeled.copy(), X2_unlabeled.copy()
    
    for iteration in range(max_iter):
        # Train both models
        model1 = base_model.__class__(**base_model.get_params())
        model2 = base_model.__class__(**base_model.get_params())
        model1.fit(X1_l, y_l)
        model2.fit(X2_l, y_l)
        
        if len(X1_u) == 0:
            break
        
        # Get confident predictions from each model
        proba1 = model1.predict_proba(X1_u)
        conf1 = proba1.max(axis=1)
        pred1 = proba1.argmax(axis=1)
        
        proba2 = model2.predict_proba(X2_u)
        conf2 = proba2.max(axis=1)
        pred2 = proba2.argmax(axis=1)
        
        # Select top n_samples_per_iter from each
        idx_conf1 = np.argsort(-conf1)[:n_samples_per_iter]
        idx_conf2 = np.argsort(-conf2)[:n_samples_per_iter]
        
        # Add to each other's training sets
        for idx in idx_conf1:
            if conf1[idx] >= threshold:
                X1_l = np.vstack([X1_l, X1_u[idx:idx+1]])
                X2_l = np.vstack([X2_l, X2_u[idx:idx+1]])
                y_l = np.append(y_l, pred1[idx])
        
        for idx in idx_conf2:
            if conf2[idx] >= threshold:
                X1_l = np.vstack([X1_l, X1_u[idx:idx+1]])
                X2_l = np.vstack([X2_l, X2_u[idx:idx+1]])
                y_l = np.append(y_l, pred2[idx])
        
        # Remove selected from unlabeled
        used = set(idx_conf1) | set(idx_conf2)
        mask = np.ones(len(X1_u), dtype=bool)
        mask[list(used)] = False
        X1_u, X2_u = X1_u[mask], X2_u[mask]
    
    return model1, model2

# === When to use co-training ===
# Best case: you have two naturally different feature sets
# e.g., image pixels + image metadata
# e.g., product title + product description
# e.g., audio signal + video frames

# === Tri-training (extension) ===
# Use 3 models, majority vote for pseudo-labels
# More robust than co-training (agreement of 2 out of 3)
# No need for feature splitting — use different bootstrap samples

sections.title: Consistency Regularization (FixMatch, MixMatch)
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: A model should predict the same label for an input even after small perturbations. If you add noise, crop, or apply data augmentation to an unlabeled image, the model's prediction shouldn't change. This “consistency” is a free supervisory signal from unlabeled data.

Key methods:

  • Pi-Model: For each unlabeled sample, apply two different augmentations. Minimize MSE between the two predictions: Lconsistency = ||f(θ(x)) - f(θ'(x))||². Simple, but both predictions can be wrong together.
  • Temporal Ensembling: Maintain an exponential moving average (EMA) of each sample's predictions across epochs. Compare current prediction to the EMA target. More stable than Pi-Model.
  • Mean Teacher: Maintain a “teacher” model whose weights are the EMA of student weights. Student predicts on augmented inputs, teacher predicts on unaugmented (or differently augmented) inputs. Student minimizes MSE to teacher. Teacher provides stable, slowly evolving targets.
  • MixMatch: Combine consistency with pseudo-labeling and MixUp. For each unlabeled sample: (1) guess label via model average, (2) sharpen the distribution (low temperature softmax), (3) MixUp between labeled and unlabeled samples with their guessed labels. State-of-the-art for semi-supervised image classification.
  • FixMatch: Simpler than MixMatch — two augmentations: weak (flip/crop) for pseudo-label, strong (RandAugment) for consistency loss. Only keep pseudo-labels with confidence > threshold. Extremely effective with as few as 10 labels per class on CIFAR-10.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# === Consistency Loss ===
def consistency_loss(student_pred, teacher_pred):
    """MSE between student and teacher predictions"""
    return F.mse_loss(student_pred, teacher_pred)

# === FixMatch loss ===
def fixmatch_loss(logits_weak, logits_strong, threshold=0.95):
    """
    logits_weak: predictions on weakly augmented unlabeled data
    logits_strong: predictions on strongly augmented unlabeled data
    """
    # Pseudo-label from weak augmentation
    probs_weak = F.softmax(logits_weak, dim=1)
    max_probs, pseudo_labels = probs_weak.max(dim=1)
    
    # Only use samples above threshold
    mask = max_probs >= threshold
    
    # Cross-entropy on strong augmentation against pseudo-labels
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits_strong, pseudo_labels, reduction='none')
    loss = (loss * mask).mean() if mask.any() else torch.tensor(0.0)
    
    return loss

# === Sharpening (for MixMatch) ===
def sharpen(probs, temperature=0.5):
    """Make distribution more confident"""
    probs = probs ** (1 / temperature)
    return probs / probs.sum(dim=1, keepdim=True)

# === Mean Teacher update ===
def update_ema(teacher_model, student_model, decay=0.999):
    with torch.no_grad():
        for teacher_param, student_param in zip(
            teacher_model.parameters(), 
            student_model.parameters()
        ):
            teacher_param.data.mul_(decay).add_(
                student_param.data, alpha=1 - decay
            )

# === Total SSL loss ===
# L = L_supervised + lambda * L_consistency
# lambda ramp-up: start small, increase over first N epochs
# Common: lambda = 0, linear ramp to 1.0 over 80 epochs

# === Practical tips ===
# 1. Weak augmentation for pseudo-labels (flip + crop)
# 2. Strong augmentation for consistency (RandAugment, CutOut)
# 3. Use EMA of model weights for inference
# 4. Labeled batch ratio: 1:1 or 1:7 (labeled:unlabeled) per mini-batch
# 5. Start with supervised-only for a few epochs

sections.title: Graph-Based Semi-Supervised Learning
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: Build a graph where nodes are data points (labeled + unlabeled) and edges represent similarity. Labels propagate through the graph — a labeled node influences its neighbors, which influence their neighbors, until labels spread to all connected nodes.

Key methods:

  • Label Propagation: Initialize labeled nodes with one-hot labels, unlabeled with zeros. Iterate: each node's label distribution = weighted average of neighbors' distributions. After convergence: unlabeled nodes have predicted distributions. Simple, effective for well-clustered data.
  • Label Spreading: Like Label Propagation but adds a clamping term (labeled nodes keep their original labels). More robust to noise. Controls influence via α parameter (higher α = more influence from neighbors vs initial labels).
  • Graph Construction: The graph structure is critical. Common: kNN graph (connect each node to k nearest neighbors), ε-ball graph (connect nodes within radius ε), or fully connected with RBF similarity. Edge weights = exp(-||x_i - x_j||² / σ²).
sections.codeBlock:
from sklearn.semi_supervised import LabelPropagation, LabelSpreading
from sklearn.neighbors import kneighbors_graph
import numpy as np

# === Label Propagation ===
# y_train has -1 for unlabeled samples
lp = LabelPropagation(kernel='knn', n_neighbors=10, max_iter=100)
lp.fit(X_train, y_train_mixed)  # y with -1 for unlabeled

# Predict on all data (including unlabeled training)
y_pred = lp.transduction_  # labels assigned during training
unlabeled_mask = y_train_mixed == -1
print(f'Unlabeled accuracy: {np.mean(y_pred[unlabeled_mask] == y_true[unlabeled_mask]):.3f}')

# === Label Spreading (more robust) ===
ls = LabelSpreading(kernel='rbf', gamma=0.5, alpha=0.8)  # alpha: clamping
ls.fit(X_train, y_train_mixed)

# Prediction probabilities
proba = ls.predict_proba(X_test)

# === Custom graph construction ===
# For better results, build a graph with domain-specific similarity
# Example: for images, use cosine similarity in feature space

def build_knn_graph(X, k=10, sigma=1.0):
    """Build kNN graph with RBF edge weights"""
    from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import pairwise_distances
    
    D = pairwise_distances(X, metric='euclidean')
    graph = np.zeros_like(D)
    
    for i in range(len(X)):
        # Find k nearest neighbors
        nearest = np.argsort(D[i])[1:k+1]  # exclude self
        graph[i, nearest] = np.exp(-D[i, nearest]**2 / (2 * sigma**2))
    
    return graph

# === When to use graph-based SSL ===
# Best: data is well-clustered, low-dimensional, with clear manifolds
# Weak: high-dimensional data (need good embeddings first)
# Use with: data where similarity metric is meaningful
# Don't use: when labeled data is extremely scarce (1-2 per class)

sections.title: Entropy Minimization
sections.description:
[Intuition] Intuition: The model should be confident about its predictions on unlabeled data. Entropy minimization encourages the decision boundary to pass through low-density regions — if the boundary goes through a dense region, many unlabeled points near the boundary would have uncertain predictions (high entropy).

Entropy minimization: Lentropy = -Σc P(c|x) · log P(c|x). For each unlabeled sample, compute the entropy of the predicted distribution. Add this as a loss term. Minimizing entropy pushes the model to make peaked (confident) predictions.

Cluster assumption: Data points within the same cluster likely share the same label. Entropy minimization + the cluster assumption means: the decision boundary should lie in low-density regions, far from any cluster center. This is why entropy minimization works — it naturally pushes boundaries to low-density gaps.

Combining with other methods: Entropy minimization is typically combined with supervised loss and consistency loss. The total SSL objective: L = Lsupervised + λconsistency · Lconsistency + λentropy · Lentropy.

Practical considerations:

  • Entropy minimization can collapse to trivial solutions (all samples to one class). Mitigation: balance with supervised loss, use a validation set to detect collapse.
  • Works best when the labeled set has at least one representative per class.
  • The entropy weight typically starts low and ramps up during training.
sections.codeBlock:
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

# === Entropy Minimization Loss ===
def entropy_loss(logits):
    """
    Entropy of predicted probabilities.
    Minimizing this encourages confident predictions.
    """
    probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=1)
    entropy = -(probs * torch.log(probs + 1e-10)).sum(dim=1)
    return entropy.mean()

# === Combined SSL Loss ===
def ssl_loss(logits_labeled, y_labeled, logits_unlabeled,
             lambda_consistency=1.0, lambda_entropy=0.1):
    """
    Combines supervised + consistency + entropy losses.
    """
    # Supervised loss (cross-entropy on labeled)
    L_sup = F.cross_entropy(logits_labeled, y_labeled)
    
    # Consistency loss (MSE between two passes with different augmentations)
    # L_con = F.mse_loss(logits_unlabeled_aug1, logits_unlabeled_aug2)
    # (computed elsewhere, passed as input)
    
    # Entropy loss (on unlabeled)
    L_ent = entropy_loss(logits_unlabeled)
    
    return L_sup + lambda_entropy * L_ent

# === Virtual Adversarial Training (VAT) ===
# A more advanced form of consistency:
# Find the perturbation direction that most changes the model output
# Minimize KL divergence between original and perturbed predictions
# This pushes the decision boundary away from data points

def vat_loss(model, x, xi=1e-6, epsilon=2.0):
    """Virtual Adversarial Training loss"""
    x.requires_grad_(True)
    logits = model(x)
    prob = F.softmax(logits, dim=1)
    
    # Random perturbation
    d = torch.randn_like(x)
    d = F.normalize(d, dim=1)
    
    # Adversarial direction (power iteration)
    for _ in range(1):
        d.requires_grad_(True)
        logits_adv = model(x + xi * d)
        prob_adv = F.softmax(logits_adv, dim=1)
        
        kl_div = (prob * torch.log(prob / (prob_adv + 1e-10) + 1e-10)).sum(1).mean()
        kl_div.backward(retain_graph=True)
        
        d = F.normalize(d.grad.detach(), dim=1)
        model.zero_grad()
    
    # Actual adversarial perturbation
    logits_adv = model(x + epsilon * d)
    prob_adv = F.softmax(logits_adv, dim=1)
    
    kl = (prob * torch.log(prob / (prob_adv + 1e-10) + 1e-10)).sum(1).mean()
    return kl

# === Practical recommendation ===
# Start with: FixMatch (best simplicity/performance trade-off)
# If you need more: MixMatch
# For text: UDA (Unsupervised Data Augmentation) with back-translation
# For graphs: Label Propagation as strong baseline
